content: Parallel to code of conduct. ("Constitution" is a very grand word, I can neither confirm nor deny the accusation that I've been reading Elinor Ostrom recently)
I propose that we organise more or less democratically by having this channel for everyone to publicly discuss how the channel is run
Currently I guess Christian is the only admin
yes, everyone can decide.
I'm not sure what I can do right now that others can't.
One superpower you definitely have is the banhammer

Maybe it's obvious, but no point leaving it unsaid.
Number 1: no racist, transphobic, homophobic, sexist language or sentiments.
Number 2: no bullying, broadly construed, eg. ridiculing questions
More generally we ought to aspire to do /better/ than the average irl conference

What's the policy on slightly invasive or awkward lines of questioning? There was some slightly gross conversation in the general intros stream along the lines of using topoi to explain women's bodies and
Brouwerism to explain "feminine mysticism" (w/e the fuck that means)
It was very offputting.
Yes. I didn't expect to need actual policies so soon within the first few hours online, we aren't quite prepared here
No worries. No one expects that kind of nonsense this quickly :P
3: This place is for math, not politics. No banning someone for stuff said elsewhere.

Mike Stay said:

3: This place is for math, not politics. No banning someone for stuff said elsewhere.

Can't agree with that, unfortunately.
As soon as we allow political discussion, the place will be taken over by politics. Politics is the mind-killer.
Yeah, I also don't agree with that.
To be extreme, if a Nazi had a solution to the RH, I wouldn't stand in the same room with them. These things are inseparable. I won't share a space for anyone with dehumanizing beliefs.
Science can not be a-political. And nothing really is. Besides, I would be happy if people wanted to start a political conversation. And I agree with Joe, I would want to exclude such a person.

Depends on what you mean by "politics". We'd have to settle on a definition. If you mean "all topics should be on the table for discussion", no, I disagree. I don't think anyone should be speculating about whether my "feminine mysticism" is brouwerian. It's essentialist, inappropriate, and I'm just going to quit instead of participating. If you mean "tit for tat policing of language", sure, I agree. But if you want to be inclusive, you have to keep in mind social context, and social context means you're going to have to interface with politics at some point, because identities are political.

I followed this drama on Twitter, where someone who was a well-known and respected speaker was discovered to be also someone posting about intelligence being related to genetics and race or something like that. It was a terrible thing to watch unfold.
Math is almost the only thing that can be apolitical. It's where I retreat from the madness of Facebook and Twitter. It would be a shame to pollute it.
if you want to discuss anything besides math/logic/computation/science/philosophy, there are plenty of places to do so besides here.

@Emily Pillmore it is a strong arc that connects identity and politics and language, can relate to the sentiment. It is hard to define rules (and I guess post-structuralism will just make everything worse)

This place is for math, not politics. No banning someone for stuff said elsewhere.

this seems to be two separate points, and it's not clear which is the point of contention (or whether both are)

I think @Christian Williams, as de facto benevolent dictator, just ruled in favor of the first point here: https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/229122-meta/topic/constitution/near/191545640

Arguments in favor of the second point so far seem to be:

  • defining acceptable political boundaries is very hard; see, e.g. Johnathan Haidt's work on how the left and right consider each other crazy or evil because of differing moral spaces. Deplatforming has historically been used by both the left and right.
  • discussions about banning someone for actions or statements based on politics/identity/deeply-held beliefs/religion/etc. outside this server will infect this server if we allow them to

Arguments against the second point seem to be:

  • Zulip does not allow blocking others' posts, so people cannot choose to ignore others whose actions or statements outside this server they find objectionable
  • many people contend that context is a necessary and integral part of all discussion, that ideas---even mathematical proof---cannot exist outside their social context, and excluding some to protect others is not only ethically justifiable but ethically required

Other arguments/corrections to what I wrote?
Can we present counter-arguments to any of the points? :P
Sure! I'm not the benevolent dictator.

Against arguments favoring the second point: 1. We can show a certain level of trust in the community that things will remain more or less cordial even in a political discussion. No hard boundaries should be necessary. 2. Bigots, sexists, toxic people are infections in and of themselves.

It is quite a responsibility to ban people. It seems a protocol and attempt at explicit rules would make things easier for the group. I would believe mediation would go a long way to get rid of misunderstandings, but beyond a certain size, online communities seem to downright attract ... "toxic people". Observable behavior should not be limited to one place, and adversity as observed in politics does not need tolerated. The trouble is "gray areas"

Joe Moeller said:

To be extreme, if a Nazi had a solution to the RH, I wouldn't stand in the same room with them. These things are inseparable. I won't share a space for anyone with dehumanizing beliefs.

There are those who say that having voted for Trump is prima facie evidence of being a Nazi. Should we exclude half the nation? What of those who voted for right-wing nationalists in European countries?

What about other contentious positions?

Example from the religiously conservative left (i.e. supports government regulation of moral questions) : Would someone who thinks that sexual relations other than heterosexual monogamy should be illegal be banned?

From the libertarian anarchist position: Would someone who openly condones the overthrow of all government be banned? Someone who believes that copyright is morally wrong and does their best to pirate all media?

Examples from the right: Would someone who takes a strong stance against abortion be banned? An American who is strongly in favor of the second amendment? Who believes in limited immigration? Who supports limited government and laissez faire capitalism? Who thinks the government should not be in a position to regulate medical care, marriage, product safety?

What about someone who opposes Israeli settlement of Palestine? Someone who thinks Taiwan is part of mainland China? Who thinks that the g factor for general intelligence is a measurable quantity? Who agrees that Seattle's math curriculum is exactly what's needed? Who supports polygamy? Who believes allowing transgender women to compete in women's sports is unfair to other women? Who thinks that humanity is a plague on the earth and the coronavirus ought to be spread as widely as possible?

Every one of the things above (well, maybe not the coronavirus guy) is politically contentious (at least in America) and utterly irrelevant to category theory.

Delete all off-topic posts. Let fall the ban-hammer for abuse and bigotry. But let's not go trawling Twitter or Facebook or personal blogs to find reasons to ban people.

Let's slow down. Clearly, this server is becoming large and lively. It will take some time for us to determine best practices. As (the present) benevolent admin, right now I ask only two things.

  1. Be kind. We share a passion, but for the most part we don't yet all know each other well. So, we'll be meeting tons of great people and sharing amazing ideas. Let's appreciate the community.

  2. Focus. We're here to discuss category theory, computation, logic, math and science and philosophy. We are free to discuss anything in our own chats and groups; but in the big streams, let's just share in the joy of learning.
Well, I'm glad I went to sleep instead of reading all that. Let me add that people who don't want to be banned for politics already have 4chan as an established place to hang out

Jules Hedges said:

Well, I'm glad I went to sleep instead of reading all that. Let me add that people who don't want to be banned for politics already have 4chan as an established place to hang out

So now 4chan is the established place for political discussion? Meaning that all internet political dicussion is alt-right, sexist and what not? Or is it that people who don't want to be banned for politics must also promote such positions?
It's a place where you definitely won't be banned for your political views

Jules Hedges said:

It's a place where you definitely won't be banned for your political views

And promotes certain extremely toxic viewpoints. So it has to be that people open for political discussion fit right in, right?

Just to be clear, I personally don't want to promote any political views or even engage in a political conversation. However, if circumstances dictate it and such a discussion emergently arises, I don't want to be banned for having such a discussion. Mocking comments such as the above are very, very low taste.
Fine, I'll say it directly then. I would be happy to have a policy to ban people, under certain circumstances, for things they say or do outside of this channel
I hope it will not come to that, and above all I'll listen to advice from people with actual experience administrating a community of this size

To be clear, I'm definitely not suggesting to ban people simply for being a supporter of Bolsonaro or whatever, in a democracy that is entirely your right. I'm talking about people who are publicly racist or transphobic or etc, and thereby make people here uncomfortable by their presence

Jules Hedges said:

To be clear, I'm definitely not suggesting to ban people simply for being a supporter of Bolsonaro or whatever, in a democracy that is entirely your right. I'm talking about people who are publicly racist or transphobic or etc, and thereby make people here uncomfortable by their presence

I'm 100% with you on this one.
It's not the same as banning/not tolerating political talk in this zulip, right?
I thought not doing pure politics here is uncontroversial. The discussion is about the scenario where one may want to ban a member for things said outside. This happened to a few communities.

So respect for the topics is, in comparison, easy. Mike has a point that we would not want to be a 'platform for deplatforming', and I'd support that but would also agree that in some special circumstances, fine to kick them out. Middle ground.

the confusion here is that there are at least 3 different topics that are all loosely described as "politics", and there's some cross-purpose talk from using different interpretations

  1. discriminatory or offensive remarks on Zulip (e.g. racism, homophobia, transphobia, etc.)
  2. discriminatory or offensive remarks elsewhere (e.g. a user who is known to be openly racist, etc.)
  3. off-topic discussion, including political discussion, which does not relate to category theory (or related areas)
(deleted)
I think no-one disagrees that the first point should be a bannable offense
Indeed.
and there seems to be a general consesus to discouraging the last point
Yep.
but perhaps there is some contention over point 2?
Not for me, no. Same policy as 1, ban them.

Yes, contention specifically: would you ban somebody who is well behaved and respected, but then is "discovered" to show problematic behavior that makes people uncomfortable here. It is sadly not a theoretical problem.

Burak Emir said:

Yes, contention specifically: would you ban somebody who is well behaved and respected, but then is "discovered" to show problematic behavior that makes people uncomfortable here. It is sadly not a theoretical problem.

I would be happy to leave it up to the community to ban said person.

it would be prudent to select a small group of moderators, trusted by the community, who are given the authority to ban people from the server — and then they can make the difficult judgement calls, if (or when) they occur
many online communities have moderation teams, for instance
Yes, something like this, however the discussion is very pertinent to them: you don't want the poor moderators have to discuss what the principle is...
I think the general rules ought to be decided upon in public, yes
W.r.t. to the 3d point, do people want to avoid political discussion at all costs, or is it in general off-topic discussions that need to be avoided?
but you don't want to get a community consensus every time a sensitive decision like that is made
@Stelios Tsampas: that's a good point: that should really be split into two points
though if off-topic discussion is discouraged in general, it also covers the politics question
@Nathanael Arkor yes, a group of moderators whose job is hopefully made easier by rough guidance from community.
With that guidance being discussed beforehand.

Nathanael Arkor said:

Stelios Tsampas: that's a good point: that should really be split into two points

Think so too. There seems to be a negative disposition specifically towards political talk.

The off-topic discussion problem can be easily "solved", by the way. I would say members should be able to create off-topic streams that do not auto-subscribe other users, so that others must explicitly join if they want to hear that kind of stuff. And yes, the same thing can be applied for political discussion. Unless people really do not want any political discussion around.

the issue with political conversation, as opposed to general off-topic conversation, is that it can have a tendency to cause friction — which is why I could see some people wanting not even to have a separate stream for that kind of discussion
we could have an "off topic" stream; but alternatively, if people want to have off-topic conversations, they could always create private conversations
there's an argument towards keeping the streams CT focused
but that's a decision that would have to be made
Personally I'd prefer to allow things to get off topic especially in #general. Lots of folks have had their usual routines totally wiped out and are stuck by themselves

Nathanael Arkor said:

the issue with political conversation, as opposed to general off-topic conversation, is that it can have a tendency to cause friction — which is why I could see some people wanting not even to have a separate stream for that kind of discussion

Exactly, that is what I suspect as well. My personal opinion is that, if someone feels it's important to talk about a certain world-wide problem, they shouldn't be discouraged on the grounds of it potentially causing friction. I want to have the possibility to talk about such things in any community. Again, I'll gladly accept the general cocensus.
If people want to let off steam about their annoying flatmate or the lack of toilet paper, I'm not going to stop them
Hahahah yes please!
How about allowing small-scale, civil political discussion, but kindly request the members to take it to the private messages if the convo gets too big or too heated?

Personally I'd prefer to allow things to get off topic especially in #general

I'm not opposed to allowing off-topic conversation somewhere, but if it is allowed, I think we should have a separate stream for it, rather than using #general, which I think makes sense to keep focused on "general-interest" discussions and notices — some people won't want to be involved in that discussion, but opting out of #general also excludes other on-topic threads
Sorry everyone, but instead of reinventing the wheel can we just copy-paste Haskell CoC, which worked flawlessly for decades?

--- This code of conduct is strongly inspired by #haskell code of conduct on FreeNode. Any suggestion is appreciated ---
The ACT channel is a very friendly, welcoming place to hang out, teach and learn. The goal of ACT is to encourage learning and discussion of applied category theory and category theory in general. As part of this we welcome newbies, and encourage teaching.
This is not just a homework group, and technical, research-related questions are also encouraged. If a question results in a very long thread though, it is probably wiser to move discussion elsewhere for interested parties.

To maintain a friendly, open culture, the following is required:

  • Low to zero tolerance for ridiculing questions. Insulting users is unacceptable. New users should feel entirely comfortable asking questions.
  • Helpful answers should be encouraged. Acknowledgements in public may act as a reward for providing a good answer.
  • Avoid getting frustrated by negative comments and ambiguous questions. Approach them by asking for details (i.e. Socratic questioning), rather than challenging the competence of the writer (ad hominem). As the group grows, we see a diverse range of people with different backgrounds getting accustomed to ACT. Be patient and take satisfaction from spreading knowledge.

-- If you have any suggestion regarding the CoC just message any of the moderators in this group. -

There is literally zero reason for reinventing (again) something that has been already reinvented countless times since more or less the internet is born. I'd say let's adopt that CoC (or some equivalent one) and then edit it as problems come up (if they will indeed come up).

Burak Emir said:

I thought not doing pure politics here is uncontroversial. The discussion is about the scenario where one may want to ban a member for things said outside. This happened to a few communities.

This is the only point I am concerned with. Nothing's off-topic in "general"; there's already at least one philosophy stream on this server. Discuss Hegel and Marx to your hearts' content on a topic dedicated to it. Abuse and toxicity here on the server is obviously bannable, and we can use an existing code of conduct for that behavior.

The only non-obvious point it exactly what @Burak Emir said, and what Nathanael Arkor said here in point 2. There is no end to what people find offensive, and as Burak says, it is not a theoretical question. I would argue that even totalitarian dictators should be able to discuss the Grothendieck construction here. @Joe Moeller and @Stelios Tsampas would certainly disagree.

Mike Stay said:

Burak Emir said:

I thought not doing pure politics here is uncontroversial. The discussion is about the scenario where one may want to ban a member for things said outside. This happened to a few communities.

This is the only point I am concerned with. Nothing's off-topic in "general"; there's already at least one philosophy stream on this server. Discuss Hegel and Marx to your hearts' content on a topic dedicated to it. Abuse and toxicity here on the server is obviously bannable, and we can use an existing code of conduct for that behavior.

So we should be able to discuss current matters or politics in "general". That's great! I was under the impression that people were proposing disallowing such conversations everywhere.

@Fabrizio Genovese
@Mike Stay

Would it work to simply add to Fabrizio's proposal some language like

"Moderators may in rare circumstances and at the request of community decide to react to behavior that has occurred outside of this forum, in order to protect the community and preserve diversity and inclusion. "

or something like that. It looks a bit like the opposite of what you propose Mike, but at least it's qualified. I do think some validation for community members who feel that is important may be in order and does not necessarily lead to the pitfalls you described.

I think it would make more sense to just say "if stuff that's not explicitly covered by the CoC happens, moderators will consult the community and will decide what to do trying to take everyone's opinion into account"
(As you can see I'm not the best at legalese)

My approach is "we'll deal with problems as they arise". So I wouldn't be explicit about the stuff we were discussing above. This is unless someone can point out direct evidence/experience of this sort of stuff having happened in the CT community (e.g. throubles at conferences because some category theorist was invited while also being a nazi or stuff like that)
I think moderators should have the authority to make carefully considered decisions like this, but will be responsible for justifying the decision to the community
I don't think the community should weigh in on every such decision, because the community is huge, and that's not scalable
Absolutely, that's more or less what I mean by "consulting". It's not "voting"
Only a small fraction are subscribed to #meta currently. Not sure if that's a bug or a feature
For now, defo a feature.

I think, in order of priority, what we want is the following:
1 - Being friendly and welcoming. Example: I don't know what a category is and I wanna learn. I don't want to come here and being treated like an useless idiot.
2 - Avoid fragmentation. We don't want this community to split. Now, everytime you make something explicit or discuss about it, some agree and some don't. For some things disagreement may result in lots of people leaving, and schisms aren't nice. So I'd avoid discussing stuff that may cause useless fragmentation unless we are really forced to. This can happen in particular with politics-related stuff, that's why I wouldn't include "political statements" in the CoC unless strictly necessary.
So everything I'm saying can be considered as "what I'd do to honor those two points as much as I can"

Burak Emir said:

It looks a bit like the opposite of what you propose Mike

Fabrizio Genovese said:

My approach is "we'll deal with problems as they arise".

It's my prediction that those with the desire to ban those that don't agree with them will try to become moderators in order to use that power. If they can't, they will make a big scene and try to force the moderators' hands. I hope I'm wrong, but I've seen it happen repeatedly elsewhere. I would like to see some kind of check on that power.

@Fabrizio Genovese there are two examples in the FP community, both people who were well-known conference speakers. Conference organizers were pressured to make a call. Shall I dig out names for you? I do agree one would want to be pragmatic and not overdo this, it's a bit draining but then if it helps avoid catastrophe later. I find we have a wonderful thing going on here.

Burak Emir said:

Fabrizio Genovese there are two examples in the FP community, both people who were well-known conference speakers. Conference organizers were pressured to make a call. Shall I dig out names for you? I do agree one would want to be pragmatic and not overdo this, it's a bit draining but then if it helps avoid catastrophe later. I find we have a wonderful thing going on here.

Yes, I am aware of the example you are referring to, and I know the names already. It was a really ugly situation, but I wouldn't call it a catastrophe. Also, having this stuff clearly written on the CoC wouldn't have avoided all the drama. The kicked out speakers would have complained anyway, moderators would still have had to defend their choice etc. etc. etc.
Agree!
It isn't a discussion about the code of conduct, but the scope of the code of conduct.
I do not insist on continued discussion and consensus. It was an opportunity to discuss a problem before it happens.

I am not opposed to it if people feel like they want to discuss about this. I just don't think much will be decided, and this kind of discussion can become very heated very fast, that's why I usually try to avoid it. But if you feel it really is importantby all means continue discussing it!
